item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, the title is "411 days a year", but 365+47=412. What happened to the 412th day, or are you intentionally pulling a Theta-prime?

Also, in a sense, this is Broken Masquerade on lite and on steroids. One employee's doing is enough to irreparably damage secrecy, but the public is now apparently anti-Foundation instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As I read it, it's more that everything that happened in Liberary never actually happened in baseline reality, so it has no effect on it (except Paul dying, of course). Liberary is beholden to the state of baseline reality, though, so while people can remember last Liberary, none of its events occurred in baseline, so they don't show up in the current iteration.

It's like the whole month is a copy of a document that you can doodle on as much as you want, and then throw away without having made any changes to the original. Any change to the original, though (the passage of time in baseline reality) will show up in the next copy.

So it's less that everyone is literally immortal, and more that they're immortal for practical purposes, because even if they died during Liberary, it never happened in our actual timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, it's like everyone and everything is immortal in Liberary then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, you're correct. He survived during SCP-3455 time and then died on what would have been the same date in baseline time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, at this moment I'm translating your article for Russian Branch and I have one more question:

Pope John Paul II did not die during this iteration

lead to his death in baseline time beginning before SCP-3455

I thought SCP-3455 occurs firtstly, then timeline resets, therefore Pope didn't die during SCP-3455 and died after it. Or do you mean that Pope technically died before next iteration?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Gah, no you're right. The dates on this were originally different. I changed them pretty late into writing it and completely missed this issue. I've removed the line about Mount St. Helens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3455 lasts for exactly 47 days, concluding at 23:59 GMT on May 3rd.

Mount St. Helens did not erupt during SCP-3455 as it did in baseline time.

On May 18, 1980, a major volcanic eruption occurred at Mount St. Helens

Have I missed the point?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Curious thing. Having time regress to nothing throughout a whole month is quite a wide change.

However, I am fairly certain there are plenty of other skips that adopt the concept of "observe, do not approach". I'd have to scrape my rusty memory for exact examples, but there are such objects that do not hoist an esoteric Archon class. So… why bring it up if things already work fine without it? It's Euclid at best in my eyes (the only guarantee that Liberary's havoc never spreads into real time are a single out-of-timer's words - unpredictable behaviour, unknown consequences, classic Euclid procedure), Keter at worst (epending on how you position yourself on the precedent of maintaining secrecy).

Also, this part positively irks me. "An unknown form of energy storm formed over the South Pacific Ocean on Liberary 41st and began moving west towards Australia, leaving a temporal void in its wake - A dark region of space in which time either does not exist or does not move." And… that's it? You're literally just skimming over a posibily catastrophic breach of containment and leave not even a faux caption describing the event. This could possibly be an object or event of interest in itself, and this documents skims over it: "Yeah, we got a timestorm over there, some people died, no big deal." Yes the heck it's a big deal!

Novote. Interesting idea, but feels a bit raw in places.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's a really interesting concept, love what you did with it. Plus, it made me discover Archon Class, which is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Basically, it is a period of time that occurs annually, and at the end of it, everything that happened over the course of the month is erased. Someone can die in Liberary, and once Liberary ends, they'd go back to however they were prior to Liberary.

No one can remember the events of Liberary outside of Liberary, but once Liberary begins, everyone remembers everything that happens in past Liberarys - even if they died in a previous Liberary.

It's world-wide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really get this one. So, it's an extra month that nobody remembers happening? Also, who is/isn't affected by it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was great, Mortos!

Also, shout out for using my hometown with the Shakespear dolphins lol. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I’m in love with this article.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this concept a lot, probably due to my minor obsession with movies like Groundhog Day and Edge of Tomorrow, and I liked the execution of it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- I had thought about Heimal, but that is specifically about anomalies containing other anomalies whereas these (and the original Archon) is just about things going really badly when containing a single anomaly.
- Never played any Persona games, so no, not intentional.
- There is actually a historical Roman festival called Liberalia (which I also managed to work in, independant of the month name) which starts on the date the anomaly itself starts on, but as far as I know Liber has never had an actual month named after him.
- No significance with the interview name; just the first name that came into my head when thinking of a name for the interviewer.

ETA: just realised I didn't reply to the correct message with this post, oops! This is a reply to jjcooper0811's questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is really good! There's another article that uses a similar esoteric class, though: http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3240 is Hiemal-class, described by Niles as "putting it in a box makes other bad shit start happening". I know Pickman used Archon-class for his 001 proposal, but this might fit better as Hiemal. Up to you, and regardless, you get a +1.

Other thoughts:

- My immediate reaction was "hey, it's the Dark Hour from Persona 3 as an SCP". I'm not sure if that's what you intended or not, but I like it.
- Pure curiosity, but is there any historical basis for a month called Liberary? Like, has it ever been on any calendar?
- Is there any significance to the interviewer's name being Christchurch? Considering the religious implications of the SCP, it seems almost too apropos.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation has a hammer and meets something that is definitely not a nail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent news. Keter feels wrong somehow, and Archon fills an interesting niche in the box metaphor:

  • If you lock it in a box, leave it alone, and nothing bad will happen, then it's probably Safe.
  • If you lock it in a box, leave it alone, and you're not entirely sure what will happen, then it's probably Euclid.
  • If you lock it in a box, leave it alone, and it easily escapes, then it's probably Keter.
  • If it is the box, then it's probably Thaumiel.
  • If locking it in a box is the cause of bad things happening, it's probably Archon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah I've decided to change this back to Archon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love it. The new beginning fixes the issue of being too purge-like and means you're in the right mood to enjoy the list of notable occurrences. I do agree that the Archon class was fine if you want to change it back :)

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd love for Archon to be a more widely accepted class.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I liked Archon too. Maybe I'll change it back; shall think on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirding that I like Archon, but I'm also not complaining. This is a very good article any way you spin it — a great elaboration of an already great concept. +1 with gusto.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also noting that I think Archon was fine. Your call though!

Definitely enjoyed this, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly, I like the mental image created by the original than this one. I liked one month of everyone going bonkers instead of a one month long party.

Second, I prefer the Archon class over the Keter class as I believed the foundation is calculated enough to know to not fully contain a god as the consequences of doing so are devastating.

This is still written well, but the original works better to me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that's interesting. Any particular reason? I'm curious now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the original more, but I'll still give this a +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still think the concept is super good, and you addressed my first issue with this version, so I'm more than happy to upvote again.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Alright reposting this. Have made a bunch of tweaks and changes to it to hopefully resolve a few issues and unintentional relations to other things. If the core idea wasn't something you liked, this probably won't change your mind, but who knows.

This is for DrMagnus' birthday contest.

Thanks to Rimple, stairfallinstitute, Lixerf, drblackbox, weryillium, djkaktus and DrBleep for reading the draft.

A note on the object class: The original version of this article was Archon class, which was defined in ihp's proposal. I was developing a very similar original object class for this while writing it, but when ihp's proposal was posted shortly before the original version of this, I decided to go with Archon instead.

Image is from here https://pxhere.com/en/photo/142008

More of my stuff here.
